facts.t: Maine becomes the first state to pass a statewide law banning the sale of alcohol.
Herman Melville publishes his whaling novel Moby-Dick.
The New York Times begins publishing as a daily newspaper.
Sojourner Truth delivers her famous Ain't I a Woman speech.
The schooner America wins a race around England's Isle of Wight, founding the America's Cup.
Uncle Tom's Cabin is published, galvanizing the antislavery movement nationwide.
Franklin Pierce is elected the 14th President of the United States.
Massachusetts passes the nation's first mandatory school attendance law.
Wells Fargo is founded to serve California gold rush banking and shipping needs.
The Studebaker brothers found their wagon-making company in South Bend, Indiana.
The United States negotiates the Gadsden Purchase to buy land from Mexico for a southern railroad.
Franklin Pierce is inaugurated as president.
Levi Strauss opens a dry goods business in San Francisco that becomes Levi Strauss and Co.
Steinway and Sons is founded in New York City to build pianos.
The New York Crystal Palace exhibition opens, showcasing American industry.
Congress passes the Kansas-Nebraska Act, reigniting the fight over slavery's expansion.
The Republican Party is founded in Ripon, Wisconsin.
Henry David Thoreau publishes Walden, his account of life at Walden Pond.
Commodore Matthew Perry signs the Convention of Kanagawa, opening Japan to US trade.
Boston abolitionists riot after fugitive slave Anthony Burns is returned to slavery.
A mob of German and Irish immigrants clashes with police in Chicago's Lager Beer Riot over a liquor law.
Walt Whitman self-publishes Leaves of Grass.
Pro- and antislavery settlers clash in the Bleeding Kansas violence.
Massachusetts becomes the first state to legally ban school segregation.
Chicago begins building the first comprehensive sewer system in the United States.
Congressman Preston Brooks beats Senator Charles Sumner with a cane on the Senate floor.
James Buchanan is elected the 15th President of the United States.
John C. Fremont becomes the first Republican Party candidate for president.
Proslavery forces sack the free-state town of Lawrence, Kansas.
Abolitionist John Brown leads the killing of five proslavery settlers at Pottawatomie Creek.
The Supreme Court rules against Dred Scott, denying citizenship to Black Americans.
A banking crisis triggers the nationwide financial Panic of 1857.
Mormon settlers and militia massacre an Arkansas emigrant wagon train at Mountain Meadows, Utah.
Elisha Otis installs the first passenger safety elevator in a New York City store.
Harper's Weekly begins publication in New York.
Abraham Lincoln and Stephen Douglas hold their famous series of debates in Illinois.
Minnesota is admitted as the 32nd state.
John Landis Mason patents the screw-top glass jar used for home canning.
The first transatlantic telegraph cable is completed, though it fails weeks later.
New York's Central Park opens to the public.
Abolitionist John Brown raids the federal armory at Harpers Ferry, Virginia.
Oregon is admitted as the 33rd state.
Prospectors discover the silver-rich Comstock Lode in Nevada.
Edwin Drake drills the first commercial oil well in Titusville, Pennsylvania.
Tightrope walker Charles Blondin crosses Niagara Falls on a rope.
Abraham Lincoln is elected the 16th President of the United States.
The Pony Express begins carrying mail between Missouri and California.
South Carolina becomes the first state to secede from the Union.
Lincoln delivers his career-making Cooper Union speech in New York.
The Democratic Party splits into rival factions at its Charleston convention.
Confederate forces open the Civil War by firing on Fort Sumter.
Abraham Lincoln is inaugurated as president for the first time.
Southern states form the Confederate States of America.
Kansas is admitted to the Union as a free state.
The first transcontinental telegraph line is completed, ending the Pony Express.
The Battle of Antietam becomes the bloodiest single day in American history.
Lincoln signs the Homestead Act, granting free western land to settlers.
Congress passes the Revenue Act of 1862, creating a federal income tax and the internal revenue office.
The ironclads Monitor and Merrimack fight to a draw at Hampton Roads.
The Morrill Land-Grant Act funds new colleges through federal land sales.
Lincoln delivers the Gettysburg Address after the Union victory at Gettysburg.
The Emancipation Proclamation formally takes effect on January 1.
West Virginia is admitted as the 35th state after splitting from Virginia.
Deadly draft riots erupt in New York City over Civil War conscription.
Congress passes the National Banking Act to create a uniform currency.
General Sherman's army marches from Atlanta to Savannah, devastating Georgia.
Abraham Lincoln is re-elected president.
Nevada is admitted as the 36th state.
In God We Trust appears on US coins for the first time.
Admiral Farragut orders damn the torpedoes at the Battle of Mobile Bay.
John Wilkes Booth assassinates President Lincoln at Ford's Theatre.
General Lee surrenders to Grant at Appomattox Court House, ending the war.
The Thirteenth Amendment abolishing slavery is ratified.
Union troops track down and kill John Wilkes Booth in Virginia.
The US Secret Service is founded, originally to fight currency counterfeiting.
The first successful permanent transatlantic telegraph cable is completed.
Congress overrides a presidential veto to pass the Civil Rights Act of 1866.
A deadly race riot breaks out in Memphis amid Reconstruction tensions.
The National Labor Union forms to advocate for an eight-hour workday.
The ASPCA is founded in New York as the first animal-welfare organization in the United States.
The United States purchases Alaska from Russia for $7.2 million.
Nebraska is admitted as the 37th state.
Congress passes the Reconstruction Acts, dividing the South into military districts.
Christopher Latham Sholes begins building an early typewriter prototype in Milwaukee.
Farmers found the National Grange to promote their economic and social interests.
The House impeaches President Andrew Johnson, who survives Senate conviction by one vote.
The Fourteenth Amendment, guaranteeing citizenship and equal protection, is ratified.
Ulysses S. Grant is elected the 18th President of the United States.
The first official nationwide Memorial Day observance honors Civil War dead.
Sholes, Glidden, and Soule patent the typewriter that Remington will later manufacture.
Workers drive the golden spike at Promontory Summit, completing the transcontinental railroad.
Wyoming Territory grants women the right to vote, a US first.
Rutgers beats Princeton in the first intercollegiate American football game.
Ulysses S. Grant is inaugurated as president.
The Knights of Labor is founded as one of the first major US labor organizations.
The Fifteenth Amendment, barring race-based voting restrictions, is ratified.
John D. Rockefeller founds Standard Oil.
Hiram Revels of Mississippi becomes the first Black member of the US Senate.
Congress establishes a national weather service to issue storm warnings.
Inventor John Wesley Hyatt develops celluloid, an early plastic, in the United States.
The Great Chicago Fire kills hundreds and destroys much of the city.
The National Rifle Association is founded in New York.
The US and Britain sign the Treaty of Washington, settling Civil War-era claims.
Showman P.T. Barnum launches the traveling circus that made him famous.
The Hayden survey explores the Yellowstone region, spurring calls to protect it.
Yellowstone becomes the world's first national park.
Susan B. Anthony is arrested for illegally voting in the presidential election.
Ulysses S. Grant is re-elected president.
Montgomery Ward launches the first mail-order catalog business.
The Credit Mobilier scandal implicating congressmen and railroad insiders breaks.
A stock market crash touches off the Panic of 1873 and a long depression.
The Coinage Act of 1873 ends the minting of silver dollars, angering silver miners.
Jacob Davis and Levi Strauss patent riveted blue jeans.
Congress passes the Comstock Law banning obscene material from the mail.
Susan B. Anthony is fined $100 for voting and publicly refuses to pay it.
The Woman's Christian Temperance Union is founded to fight alcohol abuse.
Joseph Glidden patents a practical barbed wire design.
The Philadelphia Zoo opens as the first zoo in the United States.
Buffalo hunters and Comanche warriors clash at the Second Battle of Adobe Walls.
Educators launch the Chautauqua movement of adult summer learning.
The Whiskey Ring scandal exposes distillers bribing officials to dodge liquor taxes.
The first Kentucky Derby is run at Churchill Downs.
Congress passes the Civil Rights Act of 1875, banning discrimination in public places.
Mary Baker Eddy publishes Science and Health, founding Christian Science.
Andrew Carnegie opens his Edgar Thomson steel works near Pittsburgh.
